description:
base_branch: main
integration_branch: integration/eval-pipeline
## Intent

Upgrade the evaluation pipeline from single-turn regex matching to a three-tier system that measures real Gas Town agent competence. The current role_bench is a toy — it tests whether a response contains literal command strings. Real agent work involves thousands of turns, error recovery, escalation decisions, and artifact production.

This convoy builds:
1. LLM-as-a-judge semantic scoring (fast, accurate single-turn eval)
2. Manual failure/resolution annotation tooling (data collection for trajectory eval)
3. Multi-turn trajectory evaluation from real sessions (the real benchmark)
4. Integration into the Optuna CMA-ES optimization loop

## Architecture

Three evaluation tiers, each trading speed for accuracy:

| Tier | Method | Speed | Use Case |
|------|--------|-------|----------|
| T1: Synthetic | Regex + LLM judge on canned scenarios | Seconds | Optuna inner loop |
| T2: Trajectory | Decision-point replay from annotated real sessions | Minutes | Post-Optuna validation |
| T3: Live | Full Gas Town task execution with session_scorer | Hours | Pre-deployment |

T1 exists (role_bench). This convoy builds T1 LLM judge + T2 trajectory eval + annotation tooling.
T3 is out of scope (it's the production feedback loop, already partially covered by session_scorer).

## Dependencies

- role_bench.py must exist with working score_response (DONE)
- Session transcripts at ~/.claude/projects/ (EXISTS)
- session_scorer.py for ground truth labels (EXISTS)

## Success Criteria

1. LLM judge integrated into role_bench with --judge flag
2. Annotation schema defined and tooling built for tagging failure/resolution pairs
3. Trajectory evaluator replays annotated decision points against adapters
4. Optuna objective can optionally use trajectory eval as second-stage filter
5. At least one role has 20+ annotated decision points for trajectory eval
notes:
## Community Thread

All agents working on this convoy should post updates here.
Use: bd comments add <this-id> 'your message'

## Integration Branch

This convoy uses an integration branch. All MRs target the integration branch, which lands to main atomically when the convoy completes.
title: Evaluation Pipeline: Multi-Tier Bench + LLM Judge + Trajectory Replay

description:
base_branch: main
integration_branch: integration/eval-pipeline
## Intent

Add semantic scoring to role_bench so evaluation catches behavioral intent, not just literal command strings. The current regex scorer fails when a model describes the right procedure without using exact command text.

## Scope

- Add LLM judge scoring function to eval/role_bench.py
- Support --judge flag (regex|llm) to select scoring method
- Use a cheap fast model (Haiku or local) as judge
- Output same dict format so print_report and Optuna work unchanged
- Compare regex vs LLM judge scores on existing scenarios to validate

## Dependencies

- role_bench.py with score_response (EXISTS)
- API access to a judge model (Anthropic API key or local model)

## Deliverables

1. score_response_llm() function in role_bench.py
2. --judge cli flag
3. Comparison report: regex vs LLM judge on all 6 roles
title: Epic A: LLM-as-a-Judge Scorer

description:
## What

Add score_response_llm() to eval/role_bench.py that uses an LLM to semantically evaluate whether a model response exhibits expected behaviors.

## Why

The current regex scorer (score_response) fails when a model describes correct procedure without using exact command strings. Example: "I'll check for pending tasks" should match "checks hook" but regex misses it because there's no literal "gt hook".

## Inputs

- Model response text (string)
- Expected behaviors list (list[str]) — same format as existing scenarios
- Judge model API (Anthropic Haiku or configurable)

## Implementation

1. Format a judge prompt containing: role, scenario title, expected behaviors, model response
2. Ask judge to evaluate each behavior as YES/NO with brief reasoning
3. Parse structured JSON output from judge
4. Return same dict format as score_response: {behaviors: {}, matched: int, total: int, score: float}
5. Handle API errors gracefully (fall back to regex if judge unavailable)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] score_response_llm() function added to role_bench.py
- [ ] Judge prompt produces reliable YES/NO per behavior
- [ ] Output format identical to score_response (drop-in replacement)
- [ ] API key configurable via env var (ANTHROPIC_API_KEY)
- [ ] Graceful fallback to regex on API failure
- [ ] Works with --judge llm CLI flag
design: Use anthropic SDK. Judge prompt should include the role context so it understands Gas Town domain. Parse JSON from judge response. Consider structured output (tool_use) for reliable parsing.
title: A.1: Build LLM judge scoring function

description:
## What

Add --judge flag to role_bench.py main() that switches between regex and LLM scoring.

## Implementation

- --judge regex (default, current behavior, fast)
- --judge llm (semantic scoring via LLM)
- Pass selected scorer function through evaluate() loop
- Output JSON includes which judge was used

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --judge flag added to argparse
- [ ] evaluate() accepts scorer parameter
- [ ] JSON output includes "judge": "regex" or "judge": "llm"
- [ ] Default behavior unchanged (regex)
title: A.2: Wire --judge flag into role_bench CLI

description:
## What

Run both regex and LLM judge on all 6 roles' scenarios (without a trained adapter — use base model or mock responses). Compare where they agree and diverge.

## Why

Validates that the LLM judge is actually better than regex, and identifies scenarios where regex is unreliable. Divergence points show where the bench needs improvement.

## Deliverable

crew-docs/validation/judge-comparison-report.md with:
- Per-scenario agreement rate
- Cases where regex passes but LLM judge fails (false positives in regex)
- Cases where regex fails but LLM judge passes (false negatives in regex)
- Recommendations for scenario improvements

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Both judges run on all scenarios
- [ ] Comparison report written with concrete examples
- [ ] At least 3 divergence cases analyzed in detail
- [ ] Recommendations for which judge to use in which context
title: A.3: Compare regex vs LLM judge on all scenarios

description:
base_branch: main
integration_branch: integration/eval-pipeline
## Intent

Build the data collection layer for trajectory evaluation. Before we can replay decision points against adapters, we need humans (or agents) to identify and tag the moments that matter in real sessions — failures, recoveries, escalation decisions, and exceptional completions.

## Scope

- Define annotation schema (JSONL sidecar format)
- Build annotation CLI tool that presents session turns and accepts tags
- Support failure/resolution pair linking (this failure was resolved at turn N)
- Store annotations in eval/annotations/ per role
- Integrate with session_scorer.py ground truth labels

## Why Manual Annotation

Automated extraction can find tool calls and errors mechanically, but it can't identify:
- Subtle failure modes (retry loops, wrong approach, wasted effort)
- Quality of recovery (did the fix address root cause or just symptoms?)
- Escalation judgment (should this have been escalated earlier/later?)
- Exceptional performance (creative solutions worth reinforcing)

Human judgment tags these. The tags become gold-standard evaluation data.

## Deliverables

1. Annotation schema spec document
2. CLI annotation tool (eval/annotate.py)
3. At least 20 annotated decision points for mayor role (bootstrap dataset)
title: Epic B: Session Annotation Tooling

description:
## What

Design and document the annotation schema for tagging decision points in Gas Town session transcripts. This is the data format that trajectory evaluation consumes.

## Schema Design

Each annotation is a JSONL line:
```json
{
  "session_id": "abc123",
  "turn": 847,
  "type": "failure|resolution|escalation|exceptional",
  "category": "retry-loop|wrong-tool|missed-signal|late-escalation|...",
  "label": "short-slug",
  "pair_id": "links failure to its resolution",
  "context_window": [840, 855],
  "note": "human-readable explanation of what happened and why it matters",
  "role": "polecat",
  "ground_truth_action": "read stderr output",
  "actual_action": "re-ran failing test"
}
```

## Annotation Types

- **failure**: The agent made a suboptimal decision
- **resolution**: The corrective action that fixed a prior failure (linked via pair_id)
- **escalation**: An escalation decision (good or bad, noted in the note)
- **exceptional**: Unusually good performance worth reinforcing

## Categories (extensible)

- retry-loop: Agent retries same failing approach
- wrong-tool: Agent uses wrong tool for the situation
- missed-signal: Agent ignores error output, mail, or diagnostic
- late-escalation: Agent should have escalated sooner
- early-escalation: Agent escalated when it could have solved it
- creative-recovery: Exceptional problem-solving approach
- efficient-path: Agent found shortest path to solution

## Deliverables

- eval/annotations/README.md — schema spec with examples
- eval/annotations/schema.json — JSON Schema for validation

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Schema covers failure, resolution, escalation, exceptional types
- [ ] Failure/resolution pairs linkable via pair_id
- [ ] Categories are extensible (not hardcoded enum)
- [ ] Context window captures surrounding turns for replay
- [ ] Schema documented with 3+ concrete examples from real sessions
- [ ] JSON Schema for automated validation
design: Keep the schema minimal but extensible. The pair_id linking is critical — trajectory eval needs to know 'at this decision point, the bad choice was X and the good choice was Y'. Categories should be suggested, not enforced.
title: B.1: Define annotation schema and JSONL format

description:
## What

Build eval/annotate.py — a CLI tool that presents session turns and lets the annotator tag decision points.

## How It Works

1. Load a session transcript (JSONL from ~/.claude/projects/)
2. Display turns sequentially with context (role, tool calls, content preview)
3. Annotator can:
   - Skip forward/backward through turns
   - Tag current turn with type + category + note
   - Link a resolution to a prior failure (pair_id)
   - Save annotations to eval/annotations/{role}_{session_id}.jsonl
4. Summary at end: N annotations saved, N failure/resolution pairs

## CLI Interface

```bash
# Annotate a specific session
python -m eval.annotate --session ~/.claude/projects/.../session.jsonl --role mayor

# Annotate with auto-detected role from session content
python -m eval.annotate --session session.jsonl

# Resume annotation (skip already-annotated turns)
python -m eval.annotate --session session.jsonl --resume
```

## Key UX Decisions

- Show enough context per turn to make judgment (tool name, first 200 chars of content, any error indicators)
- Color-code tool calls vs text responses vs errors
- Allow batch skip (jump forward 10 turns) for boring stretches
- Auto-suggest "failure" type when turn contains error patterns

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Loads session transcripts from standard Claude JSONL format
- [ ] Displays turns with role, tool, content preview, error indicators
- [ ] Supports tag (type + category + note), skip, link, save operations
- [ ] Outputs valid annotation JSONL per schema from B.1
- [ ] Failure/resolution pair linking works
- [ ] Resume mode skips already-annotated turns
- [ ] Handles large sessions (1000+ turns) without loading everything into memory
title: B.2: Build annotation CLI tool

description:
## What

Use the annotation tool (B.2) to tag at least 20 decision points from real mayor sessions. This bootstraps the evaluation dataset for trajectory eval.

## Focus Areas

Mayor sessions are best for bootstrapping because they have the most diverse behavior:
- Cold start procedures (hook check, mail read, convoy status)
- Dispatch decisions (which polecat, which bead, what priority)
- Escalation handling (receiving escalations from witness/deacon)
- Convoy management (create, track, close convoys)
- Cross-rig coordination (mail to other rigs, formula invocation)

## Target Mix

- 8+ failure annotations (retry loops, wrong dispatch, missed mail)
- 8+ resolution annotations (paired with failures)
- 4+ escalation decision points
- Bonus: any exceptional performance worth reinforcing

## Deliverable

eval/annotations/mayor_*.jsonl files with 20+ annotations

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] 20+ annotations from real mayor sessions
- [ ] At least 4 failure/resolution pairs linked
- [ ] Annotations cover 3+ different failure categories
- [ ] All annotations valid per B.1 schema
title: B.3: Bootstrap 20+ mayor annotations from real sessions

description:
base_branch: main
integration_branch: integration/eval-pipeline
## Intent

Build the T2 evaluation tier: replay annotated decision points from real Gas Town sessions against a fine-tuned adapter and score whether it makes the right call.

A real polecat molecule run has thousands of turns. The current bench tests single turns in isolation. Trajectory evaluation tests whether the adapter has learned the multi-turn behavioral patterns that matter: error recovery, escalation judgment, tool selection, and artifact production.

## How It Works

1. Load annotated sessions (from Epic B's annotation tooling)
2. For each annotated decision point:
   a. Extract conversation context up to that turn
   b. Present context to the adapter as a prompt
   c. Generate the adapter's response
   d. Score against the known-good action using LLM judge (from Epic A)
   e. For failure/resolution pairs: verify adapter chooses resolution path
3. Aggregate scores across all decision points per role
4. Report: overall trajectory score, per-category breakdown (recovery, escalation, tool choice)

## Key Design Decisions

- **Behavioral cloning evaluation**: We're not running the agent live. We check "given this context, would you have done the right thing?"
- **LLM judge for scoring**: Regex can't evaluate trajectory decisions. The judge compares adapter output to the annotated good/bad action.
- **Subprocess isolation**: Same pattern as Optuna objective — fresh GPU context per evaluation batch.

## Dependencies

- Epic A: LLM judge (needed for scoring trajectory decisions)
- Epic B: Annotated sessions (the evaluation data)
- session_scorer.py: Ground truth labels for which outcomes were good

## Deliverables

1. eval/trajectory.py — trajectory evaluation module
2. CLI: python -m eval.trajectory --annotations eval/annotations/mayor/ --model output/adapters/mayor
3. JSON output format compatible with Optuna objective
4. Integration hook in optuna_rig/objective.py for optional T2 second-stage filter
title: Epic C: Multi-Turn Trajectory Evaluator

description:
## What

Build eval/trajectory.py — the T2 evaluation tier that replays annotated decision points against a fine-tuned adapter and scores whether it makes correct decisions.

## How It Works

1. Load annotated sessions from eval/annotations/{role}_*.jsonl
2. Load corresponding session transcripts
3. For each annotated decision point:
   a. Extract conversation context from turn 0 up to the annotated turn
   b. Format as ChatML conversation (system prompt + conversation history)
   c. Truncate to model's context window (4096 tokens, matching training config)
   d. Generate adapter's response
   e. Score using LLM judge: does the adapter's action match ground_truth_action?
   f. For failure/resolution pairs: score whether adapter picks resolution over failure
4. Aggregate: per-category accuracy, overall trajectory score
5. Output JSON compatible with Optuna objective

## Context Extraction

The critical challenge: real sessions have thousands of turns. The adapter was trained on 4096-token chunks. Context extraction must:
- Preserve the most recent N turns that fit in the context window
- Always include the system prompt
- Truncate from the beginning (oldest turns dropped first)
- Include tool call results, not just assistant text

## Scoring

For each decision point, the LLM judge compares:
- **Adapter's generated action** vs **ground_truth_action** from annotation
- Score: 1.0 (correct action), 0.5 (partially correct), 0.0 (wrong action)

For failure/resolution pairs:
- Present failure context → adapter should choose resolution path
- Score 1.0 if adapter avoids the failure mode, 0.0 if it repeats it

## CLI

```bash
python -m eval.trajectory \
  --annotations eval/annotations/mayor/ \
  --model output/adapters/mayor \
  --base-model Qwen/Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct \
  --output eval/results/trajectory_mayor.json
```

## Output JSON

```json
{
  "role": "mayor",
  "adapter": "output/adapters/mayor",
  "total_points": 25,
  "trajectory_score": 0.68,
  "by_category": {
    "retry-loop": {"correct": 3, "total": 5, "score": 0.6},
    "missed-signal": {"correct": 4, "total": 4, "score": 1.0}
  },
  "by_type": {
    "failure": {"avoided": 6, "total": 8, "score": 0.75},
    "resolution": {"matched": 5, "total": 8, "score": 0.625}
  },
  "details": [...]
}
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Loads annotations and corresponding session transcripts
- [ ] Extracts context windows respecting 4096 token limit
- [ ] Generates adapter responses at each decision point
- [ ] LLM judge scores actions against ground truth
- [ ] Failure/resolution pair evaluation works
- [ ] Per-category and per-type score aggregation
- [ ] JSON output compatible with Optuna objective parsing
- [ ] CLI with --annotations, --model, --base-model, --output flags
design: Subprocess isolation for GPU ops, same pattern as optuna_rig/objective.py. Context extraction uses tiktoken for token counting (already a project dependency). LLM judge reuses score_response_llm from Epic A.
title: C.1: Build trajectory evaluation module

description:
## What

Add optional trajectory evaluation as a second-stage filter in the Optuna objective function. After CMA-ES finds promising hyperparameters via T1 (role_bench), the top-N trials get validated with T2 (trajectory eval) for a more trustworthy score.

## How It Works

In optuna_rig/objective.py:
1. Run T1 (role_bench) as before → get role_bench_score
2. If --trajectory flag is set AND role_bench_score > threshold:
   a. Run trajectory eval on the same adapter
   b. Combine scores: final = 0.6 * role_bench + 0.4 * trajectory
3. Return combined score to Optuna

The threshold prevents wasting GPU time on trajectory eval for obviously bad trials. Only trials that pass T1 screening get the expensive T2 evaluation.

## CLI Changes

optuna_rig/study.py:
- --trajectory flag (enable T2 second-stage)
- --trajectory-threshold (minimum T1 score to trigger T2, default 0.3)
- --annotations (path to annotation directory)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Optuna objective supports optional trajectory eval
- [ ] Threshold filtering prevents unnecessary T2 runs
- [ ] Combined score weighting is configurable
- [ ] CLI flags added to optuna_rig/study.py
- [ ] Works correctly when no annotations exist (falls back to T1 only)
design: Keep T2 optional and backward-compatible. If no annotations dir exists or --trajectory not set, behavior is identical to current. The 0.6/0.4 weighting is a starting point — CMA-ES will figure out what matters.
title: C.2: Wire trajectory eval into Optuna objective as T2 filter

description:
## Gate Criteria

Review the A.3 comparison report (regex vs LLM judge). Decide:

1. Is the LLM judge reliable enough to score trajectory decisions?
2. Are there scenario categories where regex is actually better?
3. Any judge prompt improvements needed before using it for T2?

## Blocks

Epic C (trajectory evaluator) depends on confidence that the LLM judge works. B.2/B.3 (annotation tooling) can proceed in parallel — they don't need the judge.

## Decision Options

- **GO**: LLM judge is reliable, proceed with trajectory eval using it
- **ITERATE**: Judge needs prompt tuning, iterate A.1 before proceeding to C
- **HYBRID**: Use regex for some categories, LLM for others
title: Gate: Review LLM judge quality before trajectory work

description:
base_branch: main
integration_branch: integration/crew-nvim-observability

## Intent

Make claudecode.nvim crew sessions fully trackable in OTel telemetry and lora_forge training data, on par with how polecat/CLI sessions are tracked today.

Currently, crew sessions launched from Neovim have zero GT_* environment variables set, emit no OTel spans, and are invisible to the telemetry pipeline. The lora_forge extractor discovers the JSONL transcripts but can't distinguish them from standard CLI sessions, can't link them to OTel signals, and doesn't capture the MCP tool surface difference.

## Problem

1. All GT_* env vars are blank in crew/neovim sessions — no role, rig, harness, session ID
2. No OTel spans emitted — session is invisible to dashboards and scoring
3. lora_forge extractor treats MCP-based sessions (60+ prism-nvim tools) identically to standard CLI sessions
4. Training data metadata has no runtime_type field — can't weight or filter by execution environment
5. Claude Code UUID session.id != gt.session — can't join Claude metrics to GT telemetry

## Scope

### sfgastown rig (separate integration branch)
- GT binary: crew session env injection for neovim-launched sessions
- New harness value: claudecode-nvim (distinct from claudecode CLI)
- Grafana dashboard filters for harness type

### lora_forge rig (this integration branch)
- Extractor: detect prism-nvim MCP progress events → tag runtime_type
- Scorer: carry runtime_type through scoring pipeline
- Formatter: add runtime_type to ShareGPT metadata
- Quality filter: runtime-aware filtering (MCP sessions have different signal patterns)

## Architecture

The fix has two layers:
1. **Identity injection** (sfgastown): Set GT_* vars so OTel spans are emitted
2. **Runtime detection** (lora_forge): Detect runtime from session JSONL even without OTel linkage (graceful degradation)
title: Crew/Neovim Runtime Observability

Evaluate the mayor adapter with two methods and compare.

Method 1 - Regex baseline:

- This is the reproducible baseline

Method 2 - LLM judge:
- Run same role_bench scenarios through frontier model (Claude API) as semantic judge
- Record semantic quality scores

Comparison analysis:
- Where do regex and semantic scores agree? (validated scenarios)
- Where do they diverge? (signal about where role_bench needs improvement)
- The divergence map is valuable for improving eval quality in Phase 3

description:
Write convoy-compose-training.formula.toml -- the meta-formula that composes future training convoys.

This is the payoff of the entire convoy. Given a set of roles and a training goal, this formula:
1. Assess: which roles need retraining? (data volume, adapter age, eval scores)
2. Plan: per-role training config (score threshold, dataset size, GPU budget)
3. Materialize: create beads for each roles training run
4. Convoy: track all training beads in a single convoy
5. Gate: human reviews the plan before dispatch

This formula composes the building blocks from B.1-B.3. Future training cycles use this instead of hand-designed convoys.

Inputs: All B.1-B.3 formulas exist and are validated, Epic D proves pipeline works
Outputs: .beads/formulas/convoy-compose-training.formula.toml

- **Coverage**: 100% — all 281 sessions scored, no heuristic fallback needed

close_reason: MCP tool calls now carry source attribution (source=mcp/standard, mcp_server=prism-nvim/etc) in extracted data and XML tags. Verified: 18251 tool calls across 313 sessions, 494 MCP (prism-nvim=252, context-mode=224, claude-mem=18). Design: preserve MCP names, don't normalize.
description:
## What

Distinguish MCP tool calls from standard tool calls in training data so the model learns the correct tool surface for each runtime.

## Problem

Currently all tool calls are formatted as <tool_call name="...">. An MCP call like mcp__prism-nvim__edit_buffer looks identical in structure to a standard Edit call, but they have different semantics, availability, and should potentially be weighted differently.

## Approach

- Tag tool calls with their source: standard, mcp-prism, mcp-context-mode, mcp-other
- Optionally normalize MCP tool names to their semantic equivalent for training (mcp__prism-nvim__edit_buffer → Edit with mcp_source attribute)
- Preserve the distinction in metadata so training can filter/weight

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Tool calls carry source attribution in extracted data
- [ ] MCP server name preserved from mcp_progress events
- [ ] Training data format supports tool source metadata
- [ ] Design decision documented: normalize vs preserve MCP names
title: Add MCP tool call discrimination to training data

Build a tool (Go or Python) that queries VictoriaLogs via LogsQL, reconstructs full agent conversations from telemetry events (correlated by run.id), and produces OpenAI chat JSONL training files. description:
Beads created for the training foundation convoy had minimal descriptions -- just a sentence or two. When a polecat picks up a task, the bead IS its context. Thin beads force agents to guess, explore, or do the wrong thing.

Every task bead dispatched to a polecat needs:
- What to build/do (specific, not vague)
- Why (how it fits in the convoy/epic)
- Inputs (what exists, where to find it)
- Outputs (what to produce, where to put it)
- Acceptance criteria (how to know it's done)
- Dependencies context (what upstream work produced)

This is a systemic issue -- bead authoring needs a quality bar before staging.
title: Observation: Thin beads lack context for autonomous agent work

description:
Build the score composer that aggregates multi-level signals into a single quality score per session.

Module - data/transform/session_scorer.py:
- Takes a session with linked bead_id and OTel signals (from A.1 session_linker output)
- Computes scores at three levels:
  - Turn-level: tool success rate, error recovery patterns
  - Step-level: artifact production, escalation count
  - Formula-level: completion rate, duration vs role median
- Composes into single quality_score via configurable weights dict
- Weights are the telemetry recipe -- the vector CMA-ES will later optimize (Phase 3)
- Priority cascade for signal sources: OTel signals -> bead lifecycle -> events trail -> heuristic
- Must work even when OTel signals are empty (heuristic fallback)

Inputs: Wave 1 findings, A.1 interface contract (session_id -> {bead_id, otel_signals})
Outputs: data/transform/session_scorer.py
Dependencies: None beyond stdlib (consumes A.1 output dict, no direct OTel queries)
design: Part of Epic A (Session Scorer). Consumes A.1 output. Consumed by A.3 integration. The weights dict is deliberately simple -- CMA-ES (Phase 3 Optuna rig) will optimize it later. Ship reasonable defaults now.

close_reason: Refinery running (lf-refinery), Deacon alive (hq-deacon), queue null
description:
Check refinery and deacon health.

**Step 1: Check refinery session**
```bash
gt session status <rig>/refinery
```

If MRs waiting AND refinery not running:
```bash
gt session start <rig>/refinery
gt mail send <rig>/refinery -s "PATROL: Wake up" -m "Merge requests in queue. Please process."
gt mol step emit-event --channel refinery --type PATROL_WAKE \
  --payload source=witness --payload queue_depth=<N>
```

**Event emission**: Always emit a file event when waking the refinery.
This ensures the refinery's `await-event` unblocks instantly instead of
waiting for its next timeout cycle.

**Step 2: Queue health analysis**

Run the full queue view to get raw data for every open MR:
```bash
gt refinery ready --all --json
```

This returns all open MRs with timestamps, assignees, and branch existence data.
Use your judgment to assess the queue — there are no hardcoded thresholds.

**What to look for:**

- **Stale claimed MRs**: MRs with a non-empty `Assignee` but old `UpdatedAt`.
  Consider the queue size, time of day, and typical processing time.
  A claimed MR that hasn't been updated in a while may indicate a stuck refinery.

- **Orphaned branches**: MRs where both `BranchExistsLocal` and `BranchExistsRemote`
  are false. The source branch may have been deleted while the MR bead is still open.
  These likely need to be closed or investigated.

- **Queue depth**: A large number of unclaimed MRs may indicate the refinery is down
  or overwhelmed. Consider waking it or escalating.

**Step 3: Check deacon health**

⚠️ **The deacon tmux session is named `hq-deacon`** (NOT `deacon`).
Town-level agents use the `hq-` prefix.

```bash
tmux has-session -t hq-deacon 2>/dev/null && echo "alive" || echo "dead"
```

If the deacon session is dead, escalate to Mayor:
```bash
gt mail send mayor/ -s "ALERT: Deacon session hq-deacon is down" \
  -m "Deacon tmux session (hq-deacon) not found.
Detected during witness patrol.
Please restart the deacon."
```

**Step 4: Escalate if needed**

If you identify problems, escalate to Deacon with specific MR IDs and context:
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "QUEUE_HEALTH: <summary>" \
  -m "MR IDs: <ids>
Observation: <what you found>
Recommendation: <what should happen>"
```
title: Check refinery and deacon health

description:
Check the beads merge queue - this is the SOURCE OF TRUTH for pending merges.

```bash
git fetch --prune origin
gt mq list <rig>
```

The beads MQ tracks all pending merge requests. Do NOT rely on `git branch -r | grep polecat`
as branches may exist without MR beads, or MR beads may exist for already-merged work.

If queue empty, skip to "check-integration-branches" step.

For each MR in the queue, verify the branch still exists:
```bash
git branch -r | grep <branch>
```

If branch doesn't exist for a queued MR:
- Close the MR bead: `bd close <mr-id> --reason "Branch no longer exists"`
- Remove from processing queue

Track verified MR list for this cycle.

If any notifications, closures, or archiving were missed, do them now!

title: Generate handoff summary

description:
Per-rig worker monitor patrol loop.

The Witness is the Pit Boss for your rig. You watch polecats, nudge them toward
completion, verify clean git state before kills, and escalate stuck workers.

**You do NOT do implementation work.** Your job is oversight, not coding.

## Ephemeral Polecat Model

Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission, recyclable immediately:

```
Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle:      created → queued → processed → merged (Refinery handles)
```

Once a polecat's branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean), the polecat can be
nuked immediately. The MR continues independently in the Refinery. If conflicts
arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution task for a NEW polecat.

**Key principle**: Polecat lifecycle is separate from MR lifecycle.

## Design Philosophy

This patrol follows Gas Town principles:
- **Discovery over tracking**: Observe reality each cycle, with minimal agent-bead state for duration tracking
- **Events over state**: POLECAT_DONE mail triggers immediate cleanup
- **Ephemeral by default**: Clean polecats are nuked immediately, no waiting
- **Cleanup wisps for exceptions**: Only created when intervention needed
- **Task tool for parallelism**: Subagents inspect polecats, not molecule arms

## Patrol Shape (Linear)

```
inbox-check ─► process-cleanups ─► check-refinery ─► survey-workers
                                                            │
         ┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
         ▼
  check-timer-gates ─► check-swarm ─► patrol-cleanup ─► context-check ─► loop-or-exit
```

No dynamic arms. No fanout gates. No persistent nudge counters.
State is discovered each cycle from reality (tmux, beads, mail).
title: mol-witness-patrol

close_reason: Inbox clean, no cleanup wisps pending
description:
Verify inbox hygiene before ending patrol cycle.

**Step 1: Check inbox state**
```bash
gt mail inbox
```

In the ephemeral model, most POLECAT_DONE messages are handled immediately
(auto-nuke) and archived. Inbox should contain ONLY:
- Unprocessed messages (just arrived, will handle next cycle)
- MERGED notifications (informational, archive after reading)

**Step 2: Archive any stale messages**

Look for messages that were processed but not archived:
- POLECAT_STARTED older than this cycle → archive
- POLECAT_DONE that was auto-nuked → should be archived already
- MERGED notifications → archive after acknowledging
- HELP/Blocked that was escalated → archive
- SWARM_START that created tracking wisp → archive

```bash
# For each stale message found:
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**Step 3: Verify cleanup wisp hygiene**

In the ephemeral model, cleanup wisps should be rare (only for dirty polecats):
```bash
bd list --label cleanup --status=open
```

- state:pending → Needs investigation in process-cleanups
- state:merge-requested → Legacy state, handle in inbox-check

If cleanup wisps are accumulating, investigate why polecats aren't clean.

**Goal**: Inbox should be nearly empty. Cleanup wisps should be rare.
title: End-of-cycle inbox hygiene

description:
Process cleanup wisps (exception handling for dirty polecats).

In the ephemeral model, cleanup wisps are only created when a polecat has
dirty state (uncommitted changes, unpushed commits) that prevented immediate
nuke. Most polecats are nuked immediately on POLECAT_DONE and never create wisps.

```bash
# Find all cleanup wisps
bd list --label cleanup --status=open
```

If no wisps, skip this step (most common case in ephemeral model).

For each cleanup wisp, investigate and resolve the dirty state:

## State: pending (needs investigation)

1. **Extract polecat name** from wisp title/labels

2. **Diagnose the problem**:
```bash
cd polecats/<name>
git status                    # What's uncommitted?
git stash list                # Any stashed work?
git log @{u}..HEAD     # Any unpushed commits?
```

3. **Resolution options**:
   - **Uncommitted changes**: Commit and push, then nuke
   - **Stashed work**: Pop and commit, or discard if not valuable
   - **Unpushed commits**: Push to origin, then nuke
   - **All valuable work lost**: Escalate to Deacon for recovery

4. **If resolvable locally**: Fix and nuke
```bash
# Example: push unpushed commits
git push origin HEAD

# Then nuke
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Close the wisp
bd close <wisp-id> --reason "Resolved: pushed commits, nuked"
```

5. **If needs escalation**: Send RECOVERY_NEEDED to Deacon
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "RECOVERY_NEEDED <rig>/<polecat>" \
  -m "Cleanup Status: <status>
Branch: <branch>
Issue: <issue-id>

Cannot auto-resolve. Please advise."
```
Leave wisp open until Deacon resolves.

## State: merge-requested (legacy, rare)

This state was used before the ephemeral model. If found, the polecat is
waiting for a MERGED signal. The inbox-check step handles these.

**Parallelism**: Use Task tool subagents to process multiple cleanups concurrently.
Each cleanup is independent - perfect for parallel execution.
title: Process pending cleanup wisps

description:
End of patrol cycle decision. Use the signals from context-check to decide.

**If you decide to continue patrolling:**

Use await-event to subscribe to the refinery event channel with exponential backoff:

```bash
gt mol step await-event --channel refinery --agent-bead gt-<rig>-refinery --backoff-base 30s --backoff-mult 2 --backoff-max 5m --cleanup
```

This command:
1. Watches `~/gt/events/refinery/` for event files (polling-based)
2. Returns IMMEDIATELY when an event is emitted (MERGE_READY, PATROL_WAKE, MQ_SUBMIT)
3. If no events, times out with exponential backoff:
   - First timeout: 30s
   - Second timeout: 60s
   - Third timeout: 120s
   - ...capped at 5 minutes max
4. Tracks `idle:N` label on refinery agent bead for backoff state
5. `--cleanup` auto-deletes processed event files

**Supported events:**
- `MERGE_READY` — from witness when polecat branch is pushed and ready to merge
- `PATROL_WAKE` — from witness when MRs waiting but refinery appears idle
- `MQ_SUBMIT` — from polecat via `gt mq submit`

**On event received** (refinery-specific activity):
Reset the idle counter and start next patrol cycle:
```bash
gt agent state YOUR_AGENT_BEAD --set idle=0
```

**On timeout** (no events):
The idle counter was auto-incremented. Continue to next patrol cycle
(the longer backoff will apply next time).

After await-event returns (either by event or timeout):
1. **Re-assess session health** (check RSS, context, age again — conditions change)
2. Squash the current wisp:
```bash
gt mol squash --jitter 10s --summary "<patrol-summary>"
```
3. Create and hook a new patrol wisp:
```bash
NEW_WISP=$(bd mol wisp mol-refinery-patrol --json | jq -r '.new_epic_id')
bd update "$NEW_WISP" --status=hooked --assignee=<rig>/refinery
```
4. Continue executing from the inbox-check step of the new wisp

**If you decide to hand off:**

Squash wisp with summary digest, then use `gt handoff` for clean session transition:

```bash
gt handoff -s "Patrol complete" -m "Merged X branches, Y tests passed.
Queue: empty/N remaining
RSS: X MB, Session age: Xh
Next: [any notes for successor]"
```

`gt handoff` sends handoff mail to yourself, respawns with a fresh Claude instance,
SessionStart hook runs gt prime, and your successor picks up from the hook.

**DO NOT just exit.** Always use `gt handoff` for proper lifecycle.

**IMPORTANT**: Never sleep-poll manually (e.g., `sleep 30 && bd list`).
Always use `gt mol step await-event` — it's event-driven and tracks backoff state.
title: Burn and respawn or loop

description:
End of patrol cycle decision.

**If context LOW** (can continue patrolling):

Resolve your agent bead ID for this patrol cycle. You MUST replace `<YOUR_RIG>` below with your actual rig name (e.g., `beads`, `town`) before running:
```bash
bd list --type=agent --desc-contains="role_type: witness" --json | jq -r '.[] | select(.status != "closed") | select(.description | test("(?m)^\\s*rig: <YOUR_RIG>\\s*$")) | .id'
```
This must return exactly one bead ID. If it returns zero results, STOP and report an error — verify you substituted `<YOUR_RIG>` correctly. If it returns multiple results, STOP and report an error — manual disambiguation is required. Use the single resolved bead ID as YOUR_AGENT_BEAD in the commands below.

Then use await-signal with exponential backoff to wait for activity:

```bash
gt mol step await-signal --agent-bead YOUR_AGENT_BEAD \
  --backoff-base 30s --backoff-mult 2 --backoff-max 5m
```

This command:
1. Subscribes to `bd activity --follow` (beads activity feed)
2. Returns IMMEDIATELY when any beads activity occurs
3. If no activity, times out with exponential backoff:
   - First timeout: 30s
   - Second timeout: 60s
   - Third timeout: 120s
   - ...capped at 5 minutes max
4. Tracks `idle:N` label on your agent bead for backoff state

**On signal received** (activity detected):
Reset the idle counter and start next patrol cycle:
```bash
gt agent state YOUR_AGENT_BEAD --set idle=0
```

**On timeout** (no activity):
The idle counter was auto-incremented. Continue to next patrol cycle
(the longer backoff will apply next time).

After await-signal returns (either by signal or timeout):
1. Generate a brief summary of this patrol cycle
2. Squash the current wisp:
```bash
gt mol squash --jitter 10s --summary "<patrol-summary>"
```
3. Create and hook a new patrol wisp:
```bash
NEW_WISP=$(bd mol wisp mol-witness-patrol --json | jq -r '.new_epic_id')
bd update "$NEW_WISP" --status=hooked --assignee=<rig>/witness
```
4. Continue executing from the inbox-check step of the new wisp

**If context HIGH** (approaching limit):
1. Write handoff mail with notable observations:
```bash
gt handoff -s "Witness patrol handoff" -m "<observations>"
```
2. Exit cleanly - the daemon will respawn a fresh Witness session

**IMPORTANT**: You must either create a new wisp (context LOW) or exit (context HIGH).
Never leave the session idle without work on your hook.
title: Loop or exit for respawn

description:
First, clean up wisps from previous cycles (closed wisps + abandoned wisps):
```bash
bd mol wisp gc --closed --force
bd mol wisp gc --age 1h --force
```

Then check mail for MERGE_READY submissions, escalations, and messages.

```bash
gt mail inbox
```

For each message:

**MERGE_READY**:
A polecat's work is ready for merge. Extract details and track for processing.

```bash
# Parse MERGE_READY message body:
# Branch: <branch>
# Issue: <issue-id>
# Polecat: <polecat-name>
# MR: <mr-bead-id>
# Verified: clean git state, issue closed

# Track in your merge queue for this patrol cycle:
# - Branch name
# - Issue ID
# - Polecat name (REQUIRED for MERGED notification)
# - MR bead ID (REQUIRED for closing after merge)
```

**IMPORTANT**: You MUST track the polecat name, MR bead ID, AND message ID - you will need them
in merge-push step to send MERGED notification, close the MR bead, and archive the mail.

Mark as read. The work will be processed in queue-scan/process-branch.
**Do NOT archive yet** - archive after merge/reject decision in merge-push step.

**PATROL: Wake up**:
Witness detected MRs waiting but refinery idle. Acknowledge and archive:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**HELP / Blocked**:
Assess and respond. If you can't help, escalate to Mayor.
Archive after handling:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**HANDOFF**:
Read predecessor context. Check for in-flight merges.
Archive after absorbing context:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**Hygiene principle**: Archive messages after they're fully processed.
Keep only: pending MRs in queue. Inbox should be near-empty.
title: Check refinery mail

description:
Check the beads merge queue - this is the SOURCE OF TRUTH for pending merges.

```bash
git fetch --prune origin
gt mq list <rig>
```

The beads MQ tracks all pending merge requests. Do NOT rely on `git branch -r | grep polecat`
as branches may exist without MR beads, or MR beads may exist for already-merged work.

If queue empty, skip to "check-integration-branches" step.

For each MR in the queue, verify the branch still exists:
```bash
git branch -r | grep <branch>
```

If branch doesn't exist for a queued MR:
- Close the MR bead: `bd close <mr-id> --reason "Branch no longer exists"`
- Remove from processing queue

Track verified MR list for this cycle.
title: Scan merge queue

close_reason: Inbox clean - 0 messages, 0 orphaned MR beads
description:
Verify inbox hygiene before ending patrol cycle.

**Step 1: Check inbox state**
```bash
gt mail inbox
```

Inbox should contain ONLY:
- Unprocessed MERGE_READY messages (will process next cycle)
- Active work items

**Step 2: Archive any stale messages**

Look for messages that were processed but not archived:
- PATROL: Wake up that was acknowledged → archive
- HELP/Blocked that was handled → archive
- MERGE_READY where merge completed but archive was missed → archive

```bash
# For each stale message found:
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**Step 3: Check for orphaned MR beads**

Look for open MR beads with no corresponding branch:
```bash
bd list --type=merge-request --status=open
```

For each open MR bead:
1. Check if branch exists: `git ls-remote origin refs/heads/<branch>`
2. Determine `<merge-target>` using the **Target Resolution Rule** above.
3. If branch is gone, pick `<verification-target>`:
   - If `origin/<merge-target>` exists, use `<merge-target>`.
   - If `origin/<merge-target>` is missing (e.g. deleted integration branch), use `main`.
4. Verify landed work: `git log origin/<verification-target> --oneline | grep "<source_issue>"`
5. If work found → close MR with reason "Merged (verified on <verification-target>; merge target was <merge-target>)"
6. If work NOT found → investigate before closing:
   - Check source_issue validity (should be gt-xxxxx, not branch name)
   - Search reflog/dangling commits if possible
   - If unverifiable, close with reason "Unverifiable - no audit trail"
   - File bead if this indicates lost work

**NEVER close an MR bead without verifying the work landed or is unrecoverable.**

**Goal**: Inbox should have ≤3 active messages at end of cycle.
Keep only: pending MRs in queue.
title: End-of-cycle inbox hygiene

description:
First, clean up wisps from previous cycles (closed wisps + abandoned wisps):
```bash
bd mol wisp gc --closed --force
bd mol wisp gc --age 1h --force
```

Then check inbox and handle messages.

```bash
gt mail inbox
```

For each message:

**POLECAT_STARTED**:
A new polecat has started working. Acknowledge and archive.
```bash
# Acknowledge startup (optional: log for activity tracking)
gt mail archive <message-id>
```
No action needed beyond acknowledgment - archive immediately.

**POLECAT_DONE / LIFECYCLE:Shutdown**:

*EPHEMERAL MODEL*: Polecats are truly ephemeral - done at MR submission,
recyclable immediately. Once the branch is pushed (cleanup_status=clean),
the polecat can be nuked. The MR lifecycle continues independently in the
Refinery. If conflicts arise, Refinery creates a NEW conflict-resolution
task for a NEW polecat.

Polecat lifecycle: spawning → working → mr_submitted → nuked
MR lifecycle: created → queued → processed → merged (handled by Refinery)

The handler (HandlePolecatDone) will:
1. Check cleanup_status from agent bead
2. If "clean" (branch pushed): AUTO-NUKE immediately, archive mail
3. If dirty: Create cleanup wisp for manual intervention

```bash
# The handler does this automatically:
# - For clean state: gt polecat nuke <name> → archive mail
# - For dirty state: create wisp → process in next step
```

Cleanup wisps are only created when something is wrong (uncommitted changes,
unpushed commits). Most POLECAT_DONE messages result in immediate nuke.

**MERGED**:
A branch was merged successfully. This is informational in the ephemeral model
since the polecat was already nuked after MR submission.

If a cleanup wisp exists (dirty state), complete the cleanup:
```bash
# Find the cleanup wisp for this polecat
bd list --label polecat:<name>,state:merge-requested --status=open

# If found, proceed with full polecat nuke:
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Burn the cleanup wisp
bd close <wisp-id>
```
Archive after cleanup is complete.

**HELP / Blocked**:
Assess the request. Can you help? If not, escalate to Deacon:
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "Escalation: <polecat> needs help" -m "<details>"
```
Archive after handling (escalated or resolved):
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**HANDOFF**:
Read predecessor context. Continue from where they left off.
Archive after absorbing context:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**SWARM_START**:
Mayor initiating batch polecat work. Initialize swarm tracking.
```bash
# Parse swarm info from mail body: {"swarm_id": "batch-123", "beads": ["bd-a", "bd-b"]}
bd create --ephemeral --wisp-type patrol --title "swarm:<swarm_id>" --description "Tracking batch: <swarm_id>" --labels swarm,swarm_id:<swarm_id>,total:<N>,completed:0,start:<timestamp>
```
Archive after creating swarm tracking wisp:
```bash
gt mail archive <message-id>
```

**Hygiene principle**: Archive messages after they're fully processed.
Keep only: active work, unprocessed requests. Inbox should be near-empty.
title: Process witness mail

description:
Assess whether this session should continue or hand off to a fresh one.

**Gather signals:**

1. **Process memory** — check your own RSS:
```bash
ps -o rss= -p $$   # KB — divide by 1024 for MB
```

2. **Session age** — how long has this tmux session been running:
```bash
CREATED=$(tmux display-message -t $(tmux display-message -p '#S') -p '#{session_created}')
echo "Session age: $(( ($(date +%s) - CREATED) / 3600 ))h"
```

3. **Context usage** — your internal sense of how much context you've consumed.
Are you losing track of earlier conversation? Getting verbose? Repeating yourself?

4. **Work done this cycle** — how many merges, how much complexity processed.

**The principle:** Fresh sessions are cheap. Memory bloat compounds over time and
affects the entire system — other agents, Dolt, and the OS all share the same RAM.
An idle session at 1.5 GB is worse than cycling and restarting at 200 MB.

**Make a judgment call.** If multiple signals suggest you're getting heavy
(high RSS, long session, substantial context consumed), hand off. If you're
light and there's active work in the queue, continue.
title: Assess session health

description:
**Config: integration_branch_refinery_enabled = true**
**Config: integration_branch_auto_land = false**

Read the two config values above, then:

- If integration_branch_refinery_enabled = "false": Say "Integration branches disabled." Close step.
- If integration_branch_auto_land = "false": Say "Auto-land disabled, nothing to do." Close step.
  FORBIDDEN: If auto_land is false, you MUST NOT land integration branches yourself using
  raw git commands. Do not merge integration branches to the default/target branch. Do not push
  integration branch merges. The auto_land=false setting means landing requires a human
  to run `gt mq integration land` manually. Respect this boundary unconditionally.
- If BOTH are "true":
  1. `bd list --type=epic --status=open` to find epics
  2. `gt mq integration status <epic-id>` for each epic
  3. If `ready_to_land: true`: run `gt mq integration land <epic-id>`
  4. If `ready_to_land: false`: do nothing, epic work is incomplete
  Never land partial epics — ALL children must be closed first.
title: Check integration branches for landing

description:
Check refinery and deacon health.

**Step 1: Check refinery session**
```bash
gt session status <rig>/refinery
```

If MRs waiting AND refinery not running:
```bash
gt session start <rig>/refinery
gt mail send <rig>/refinery -s "PATROL: Wake up" -m "Merge requests in queue. Please process."
gt mol step emit-event --channel refinery --type PATROL_WAKE \
  --payload source=witness --payload queue_depth=<N>
```

**Event emission**: Always emit a file event when waking the refinery.
This ensures the refinery's `await-event` unblocks instantly instead of
waiting for its next timeout cycle.

**Step 2: Queue health analysis**

Run the full queue view to get raw data for every open MR:
```bash
gt refinery ready --all --json
```

This returns all open MRs with timestamps, assignees, and branch existence data.
Use your judgment to assess the queue — there are no hardcoded thresholds.

**What to look for:**

- **Stale claimed MRs**: MRs with a non-empty `Assignee` but old `UpdatedAt`.
  Consider the queue size, time of day, and typical processing time.
  A claimed MR that hasn't been updated in a while may indicate a stuck refinery.

- **Orphaned branches**: MRs where both `BranchExistsLocal` and `BranchExistsRemote`
  are false. The source branch may have been deleted while the MR bead is still open.
  These likely need to be closed or investigated.

- **Queue depth**: A large number of unclaimed MRs may indicate the refinery is down
  or overwhelmed. Consider waking it or escalating.

**Step 3: Check deacon health**

⚠️ **The deacon tmux session is named `hq-deacon`** (NOT `deacon`).
Town-level agents use the `hq-` prefix.

```bash
tmux has-session -t hq-deacon 2>/dev/null && echo "alive" || echo "dead"
```

If the deacon session is dead, escalate to Mayor:
```bash
gt mail send mayor/ -s "ALERT: Deacon session hq-deacon is down" \
  -m "Deacon tmux session (hq-deacon) not found.
Detected during witness patrol.
Please restart the deacon."
```

**Step 4: Escalate if needed**

If you identify problems, escalate to Deacon with specific MR IDs and context:
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "QUEUE_HEALTH: <summary>" \
  -m "MR IDs: <ids>
Observation: <what you found>
Recommendation: <what should happen>"
```
title: Check refinery and deacon health

description:
Merge queue processor patrol loop.

The Refinery is the Engineer in the engine room. You process polecat branches, merging them to their effective target branches one at a time with sequential rebasing.

**The Scotty Test**: Before proceeding past any failure, ask yourself: "Would Scotty walk past a warp core leak because it existed before his shift?"

## Merge Flow

The Refinery receives MERGE_READY mail from Witnesses when polecats complete work:

```
Witness                    Refinery                      Git
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGE_READY              │                           │
   │─────────────────────────>│                           │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (verify branch)                   │
   │                          │ fetch & rebase            │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (run tests)                       │
   │                          │                           │
   │                    (if pass)                         │
   │                          │ merge & push              │
   │                          │──────────────────────────>│
   │                          │                           │
   │ MERGED                   │                           │
   │<─────────────────────────│                           │
   │                          │                           │
```

After successful merge, Refinery sends MERGED mail back to Witness so it can
complete cleanup (nuke the polecat worktree).

## CRITICAL: Read Step Instructions Before Acting

Before executing ANY step, you MUST run `bd show <step-id>` and read the full
description. Steps contain **Config:** values that override default behavior.
If a config says to skip, you skip. If it specifies a command, you use that
exact command — not your own assumption. Do NOT guess what a step requires
based on the step title or your role knowledge. The step description is the
source of truth.

## Variables

| Variable | Default | Description |
|----------|---------|-------------|
| wisp_type | patrol | Type of wisp created for this molecule |
| integration_branch_refinery_enabled | true | Whether refinery merges to integration branches |
| integration_branch_auto_land | false | Whether to auto-land integration branches when epic children all closed |
| run_tests | true | Whether to run tests before merging |
| setup_command | (empty) | Setup/install command (e.g., `pnpm install`). Empty = skip. |
| typecheck_command | (empty) | Type check command (e.g., `tsc --noEmit`). Empty = skip. |
| lint_command | (empty) | Lint command (e.g., `eslint .`). Empty = skip. |
| test_command | go test ./... | Test command to run (if run_tests is true) |
| build_command | (empty) | Build command (e.g., `go build ./...`). Empty = skip. |
| target_branch | main | Default target branch for merges |
| delete_merged_branches | true | Whether to delete source branches after merge |

## Target Resolution Rule

When instructions reference `<rebase-target>`, `<merge-target>`, or `<verification-target>`,
resolve that placeholder with this rule:

- If integration_branch_refinery_enabled = "true": use MR target when present, otherwise main.
- If integration_branch_refinery_enabled = "false": always use main.

## FORBIDDEN Actions

- FORBIDDEN: Landing integration branches to the default branch via raw git commands (`git merge`, `git push`).
  Integration branches may ONLY be landed via `gt mq integration land <epic-id>`.
  This applies regardless of `auto_land` configuration. The pre-push hook enforces this.

## Step Execution Order

You MUST process steps in strict DAG order. Walk through each step sequentially,
unless you are explicitly told to skip to a step.
title: mol-refinery-patrol

description:
Check own context usage.

If context is HIGH (>80%):
- Ensure any notes are written to handoff mail
- Prepare for session restart

If context is LOW:
- Can continue patrolling
title: Check own context limit

description:
If Mayor started a batch (SWARM_START), check if all polecats have completed.

**Step 1: Find active swarm tracking wisps**
```bash
bd list --label swarm --status=open
```
If no active swarm, skip this step.

**Step 2: Count completed polecats for this swarm**

Extract from wisp labels: swarm_id, total, completed, start timestamp.
Check how many cleanup wisps have been closed for this swarm's polecats.

**Step 3: If all complete, notify Mayor**
```bash
gt mail send mayor/ -s "SWARM_COMPLETE: <swarm_id>" -m "All <total> polecats merged.
Duration: <minutes> minutes
Swarm: <swarm_id>"

# Close the swarm tracking wisp
bd close <swarm-wisp-id> --reason "All polecats merged"
```

Note: Runs every patrol cycle. Notification sent exactly once when all complete.
title: Check if active swarm is complete

description:
Check for expired timer gates and escalate as needed.

Timer gates are async wait conditions with a timeout. When the timeout expires,
the gate should be escalated to the overseer for human intervention.

**Step 1: Run timer gate check**
```bash
bd gate check --type=timer --escalate
```

This command:
1. Finds all open gate issues with await_type=timer
2. Checks if `now > created_at + timeout`
3. Escalates expired gates via `gt escalate` (HIGH severity)
4. Reports summary of gate status

**Step 2: Review output**

If expired gates were found and escalated:
- The escalation creates an audit trail bead
- Overseer will be notified via mail
- Gate remains open until manually resolved

If no expired gates:
- Continue patrol normally

**Note**: Timer gates do NOT auto-close on expiration. They escalate.
This ensures human oversight of timeout conditions.

**Parallelism**: This is a single command, no parallel execution needed.
title: Check timer gates for expiration

close_reason: No cleanup wisps found - ephemeral model working correctly
description:
Process cleanup wisps (exception handling for dirty polecats).

In the ephemeral model, cleanup wisps are only created when a polecat has
dirty state (uncommitted changes, unpushed commits) that prevented immediate
nuke. Most polecats are nuked immediately on POLECAT_DONE and never create wisps.

```bash
# Find all cleanup wisps
bd list --label cleanup --status=open
```

If no wisps, skip this step (most common case in ephemeral model).

For each cleanup wisp, investigate and resolve the dirty state:

## State: pending (needs investigation)

1. **Extract polecat name** from wisp title/labels

2. **Diagnose the problem**:
```bash
cd polecats/<name>
git status                    # What's uncommitted?
git stash list                # Any stashed work?
git log @{u}..HEAD     # Any unpushed commits?
```

3. **Resolution options**:
   - **Uncommitted changes**: Commit and push, then nuke
   - **Stashed work**: Pop and commit, or discard if not valuable
   - **Unpushed commits**: Push to origin, then nuke
   - **All valuable work lost**: Escalate to Deacon for recovery

4. **If resolvable locally**: Fix and nuke
```bash
# Example: push unpushed commits
git push origin HEAD

# Then nuke
gt polecat nuke <name>

# Close the wisp
bd close <wisp-id> --reason "Resolved: pushed commits, nuked"
```

5. **If needs escalation**: Send RECOVERY_NEEDED to Deacon
```bash
gt mail send deacon/ -s "RECOVERY_NEEDED <rig>/<polecat>" \
  -m "Cleanup Status: <status>
Branch: <branch>
Issue: <issue-id>

Cannot auto-resolve. Please advise."
```
Leave wisp open until Deacon resolves.

## State: merge-requested (legacy, rare)

This state was used before the ephemeral model. If found, the polecat is
waiting for a MERGED signal. The inbox-check step handles these.

**Parallelism**: Use Task tool subagents to process multiple cleanups concurrently.
Each cleanup is independent - perfect for parallel execution.
title: Process pending cleanup wisps

description:
Summarize this patrol cycle.

**VERIFICATION**: Before generating summary, confirm for each merged branch:
- [ ] MERGED mail was sent to witness
- [ ] MR bead was closed
- [ ] Source issue was closed
- [ ] MERGE_READY mail archived

If any notifications, closures, or archiving were missed, do them now!

Include in summary:
- Branches merged (count, names)
- MERGED mails sent (count - should match branches merged)
- MR beads closed (count - should match branches merged)
- Source issues closed (count - should match branches merged)
- MERGE_READY mails archived (count - should match branches merged)
- Test results (pass/fail)
- Branches with conflicts (count, names)
- Conflict-resolution tasks created (IDs)
- Issues filed (if any)
- Any escalations sent

**Conflict tracking is important** for monitoring MQ health. If many branches
conflict, it may indicate target branches are moving too fast or branches are too stale.

This becomes the digest when the patrol is squashed.
title: Generate handoff summary

If cleanup wisps are accumulating, investigate why polecats aren't clean.

**Goal**: Inbox should be nearly empty. Cleanup wisps should be rare.


**IMPORTANT**: You MUST track the polecat name, MR bead ID, AND message ID - you will need them
in merge-push step to send MERGED notification, close the MR bead, and archive the mail.

**Step 1: Check inbox state**
